question: How many bytes does the string 'Привет' (6 Cyrillic characters) take in UTF-8?

options.explanation: 6 is the string length in characters (String.length). Each Cyrillic character takes 2 bytes in UTF-8, totaling 12
24 bytes would be UTF-32 (4 bytes per character). Cyrillic in UTF-8 takes 2 bytes
18 bytes would be 3 bytes per character. Cyrillic in UTF-8 takes exactly 2 bytes per character

options.text: The buffer may contain data from previously freed memory

options.explanation: Actually, allocUnsafe is faster — it skips zeroing memory. The danger is that old data remains

options.text: It creates a buffer outside V8 heap
options.explanation: Small allocUnsafe buffers are allocated from a pool in V8 heap. The danger is uncleared memory
allocUnsafe supports all the same encodings. The danger is uninitialized memory

options.text: Creates a view on the same memory — changes are visible in the original
Creates an independent copy of data
options.explanation: slice() returns a view on the same memory. For a copy use Buffer.from(buf.slice())
Buffer has fixed size. slice() creates a new Buffer view on part of the original memory

options.text: Splits the buffer into an array of smaller buffers
options.explanation: slice() returns a single Buffer — a view on part of the original. Splitting requires custom code
DataView is a low-level API for binary data in ArrayBuffer with byte order control
DataView is not immutable — you can write through it. Its purpose is endianness control
DataView works with numeric types (int, float), not text encodings. Use TextEncoder/TextDecoder for text

question: What byte order is used in network protocols?

options.explanation: Little-endian is used by x86/ARM CPUs. Network protocols (TCP, IP, HTTP) use big-endian
RFC 1700 defines big-endian as Network Byte Order for all internet protocols
Network Byte Order (big-endian) was adopted precisely for architecture independence
